g.data-narration: Welcome to the lecture on Bus Structure - the communication backbone of computer systems.
g.text: A comprehensive guide to computer bus architecture

g.data-narration: A bus is a shared communication pathway connecting the CPU, memory, and I/O devices with parallel wires.
g.text: A bus is a group of parallel wires that serves as a
components like CPU, Memory, and I/O devices.

g.data-narration: Understanding bus architecture is essential for designing efficient computers and diagnosing performance bottlenecks.
g.text: • Understand Data, Address, and Control buses
• Compare single vs. multiple bus structures

g.data-narration: There are three types of buses: Data bus carries actual data, Address bus carries memory locations, Control bus carries timing signals.
g.text: Three Types of Buses in a Computer System

g.data-narration: The Data Bus carries actual data between components. It's bidirectional and its width determines how much data transfers at once.
The Address Bus carries memory addresses from CPU to memory. It's unidirectional and its width determines addressable memory space.
The Control Bus carries timing and control signals like Read, Write, Memory Request, and Interrupt lines to coordinate operations.
g.text: CONTROL BUS - Carries Timing & Control Signals

g.data-narration: Single bus structure connects all components to one shared bus. It's simple but creates a bottleneck as more devices share it.
g.text: Only one transfer at a time

g.data-narration: Multiple bus structure uses hierarchical buses with bridges, separating processor, memory, and I/O buses for better performance.
A bus cycle has three phases: Address phase where CPU places address, Data phase for transfer, and Acknowledgment from device.
Synchronous buses use a common clock with fixed timing. Asynchronous buses use handshaking protocols for flexible device communication.
Bus arbitration resolves conflicts when multiple devices want the bus. Centralized uses one arbiter; distributed has logic in each device.
Bus bandwidth equals width times frequency. A 64-bit bus at 800 MHz delivers 6.4 GB/s - crucial for system performance.
Key takeaways: Buses are shared pathways, three types with different directions, single bus is simple but creates bottlenecks.
Bus concepts apply to PCIe, USB, SATA in real systems, motherboard design, SoC, and high-performance computing.
